meta.title: S205 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes C-Class S205 engine — OM651, OM654, M276, M177 and M274. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the OM651-to-OM654 diesel switch explained. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C-Class S205 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S205 engine typically costs £1,500–£14,000 supply only, depending on engine — the OM626 and M274 four-cylinders sit at £1,500–£3,500, the OM651 diesel at £2,800–£4,800, the OM654 at £3,000–£5,000, the M276 V6 at £3,800–£6,500, and the M177 V8 at £6,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £520–£1,300 depending on engine.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C-Class S205 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM654.920 diesel (post-2018 C 220d/C 300d) is our top recommendation — it's a modern, clean-sheet design that avoids the OM651's injector seal failure and timing chain tensioner issues. The M274 petrol four-cylinder is also a solid choice, with fewer major failure points than the V6 or V8.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C-Class S205?

acceptedAnswer.text: Injector seal failure ('Black Death') on the OM651 diesel (pre-2018), timing chain stretch on the M276 V6 (C 400/C 43), valve cover oil leaks on the M177 V8 (AMG C 63/S), and AdBlue system issues on the OM654 diesel (post-2018). The M274 petrol engines are generally trouble-free aside from carbon buildup on higher-mileage examples.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C-Class S205 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, but it depends heavily on which engine you choose. The OM651 diesel is the generation's biggest risk — injector seal failure is common and costly. The OM654 is a significant improvement. The M276 V6 is reliable if the timing chain is maintained; the M177 V8 is robust but has expensive oil leak issues. The M274 four-cylinder petrol is the most trouble-free option overall.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes C-Class S205 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine is an outright 'avoid', but a pre-2018 C 220d or C 250d with the OM651 and no documented injector seal replacement history is the one purchase we consistently advise against. A neglected AMG C 63 without valve cover service history is the second.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S205 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Diesel smell/chuffing (OM651 injector seals), cold-start rattle (M276 timing chain), oil smell/visible seepage (M177 valve covers), or AdBlue warning lights (OM654) are the key warning signs. Have any of these checked immediately to avoid escalation.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C-Class S205 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £10,000 S205, a £3,000 OM651 recon is 30% — safe. Against a tired £6,000 example, the same recon is 50% — borderline. For the V8, against a £25,000+ C63, a £8,000 recon is just 32% — viable. Our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold is the line.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes C-Class S205 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM651 diesel can exceed 200,000 miles with diligent maintenance, but injector seals and timing chain tensioner are the limiting factors. The OM654 is too new to have definitive longevity data, but early signs are positive. The M276 V6 and M177 V8 can easily reach 150,000+ miles with proper servicing — oil leaks are the main cost, not bottom-end failure.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S205, W205, C205 and A205?

acceptedAnswer.text: All four share the fourth-generation C-Class platform and most engines. The S205 is the 5-door estate (2014–2021, this page), the W205 is the 4-door saloon (2014–2021), the C205 is the 2-door coupé (2015–2021), and the A205 is the 2-door cabriolet (2016–2021). Costs vary slightly by body style due to labour access — see each generation's own page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C-Class S205 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate under the bonnet and in the vehicle documentation; block stamping is visible from below on most engines. Tell us your variant and approximate registration date (pre- or post-2018 facelift) and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes C-Class S205 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the C-Class estate — from the frugal OM626 four-cylinder diesel to the thunderous M177 V8 in the C63 S. Real UK replacement costs, honest reliability data, and the OM651-to-OM654 diesel switch that defined this generation. Backed by 1,250+ real UK OM651 enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes C-Class S205 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S205 estate specifically — from the entry-level diesel to the 510hp V8 that defines AMG's golden era.

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: OM651: 95% confidence — 1,250 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries in 2025; failure patterns well-documented. OM654: 85% confidence — 520 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries; newer engine with emerging pattern data. M276: 90% confidence — 390 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries; documented timing chain stretch issues. M177: 90% confidence — 370 [MB-VERIFIED] enquiries; well-understood V8. M274: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier. OM626: 70% confidence — pattern-data tier.

overview.intro: The S205 is the estate variant of Mercedes-Benz's fourth-generation C-Class (W205), launched in 2014 as the successor to the S204 estate. Built on the MRA platform, it brought significant weight savings and a more premium interior — and, with the C63 S, the most powerful estate Mercedes had ever built. A 2018 facelift introduced the new OM654 diesel engine, replacing the ageing OM651 across the range.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes-Benz MRA (Modular Rear Architecture) • Predecessor: C-Class S204 (W204 estate) • Successor: C-Class S206 (W206 estate) • Sibling body styles in this generation: W205 saloon, C205 coupé, A205 cabriolet.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The OM651 diesel engine generated 1,250 real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], making it the most-requested C-Class engine in our data as these high-mileage workhorses exit warranty.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre four-cylinder diesel that replaced the OM651 — smoother, more efficient, and without the OM651's injector seal 'Black Death' risk. AdBlue system issues are the main concern, but these are software-manageable.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern diesel without the OM651's ageing weaknesses.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 offers genuine performance with none of the M177's hot-vee heat management headaches. Timing chain stretch is its known weak point, but preventative maintenance keeps it in check.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want six-cylinder refinement without V8 running costs.

items.quote: Injector seal failure ('Black Death') is this engine's defining risk — carbon buildup around the injectors leads to compression loss and, if ignored, engine damage. Timing chain tensioner wear is a secondary concern.
items.whoItsFor: only buyers with documented injector seal replacement history.

items.quote: The 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 delivers 503bhp in S form, but the hot-vee layout concentrates heat between the cylinder banks — valve cover oil leaks and coolant system issues are the most common, with full replacement costs reaching £14,000.
items.whoItsFor: AMG buyers with deep pockets and full service history.

items.quote: The newer diesel offers genuine fuel economy (60+ mpg) with significantly lower failure rates than the OM651 it replaced — and sits in the same used price bracket as older cars with the older engine.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want a modern, efficient estate without the OM651's caveats.

items.quote: The 2.0-litre turbo petrol is often dismissed in favour of the diesel, but it offers genuine performance (6.2s to 60mph) with none of the DPF or injector issues that plague the diesels. It's the sensible petrol choice.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want petrol refinement without the V6 or V8 premium.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best V6 — performance without V8 cost.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Valve cover oil leaks, coolant system
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Premium — high power, high cost.
High Risk — budget for injector work.

economicsRule.title: The S205 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£4,800 OM651 recon against a £6,000–£10,000 S205 sits at 30–80% of the car's value — on sub-£7,000 examples, it brushes our 70% Mercedes-Benz threshold. The OM654 is a safer bet: newer cars are worth more, so the same £3,000–£5,000 recon drops to a routine 25–40%. Value the car first; budget for the engine second.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S205 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: C 200d (OM626) — cheapest recon, simple four-cylinder diesel.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S205 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: AMG C 63 S — V8 replacement and hot-vee maintenance costs.

keyTakeaways.question: Which S205 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: Post-2018 C 220d (OM654) — modern diesel without the OM651's caveats.

problems.title: OM651 Injector Seal Failure ('Black Death')
problems.rootCause: The copper injector seals degrade over time, allowing combustion gases to escape past the injector — this causes carbon buildup around the injector, a telltale "chuffing" sound, and eventually compression loss if ignored.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of chuffing or diesel smell from the engine bay, have the injector seals inspected immediately — a £400–£1,200 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £4,000+ replacement that follows if compression loss occurs.

cta.label: Compare OM651 engine prices for your S205 C 220d →

problems.title: M276 Timing Chain Stretch
problems.affectedModels: S205 C 400, C 450 AMG Sport, AMG C 43 (all M276.823 variants).
problems.rootCause: The timing chain tensioner can wear prematurely, allowing the chain to stretch — this leads to rattling on start-up and, in extreme cases, catastrophic engine failure if the chain jumps.
problems.recommendation: If your C 400 or C 43 rattles on cold start, don't delay — a £1,500–£2,500 chain replacement is a fraction of the £5,500+ engine replacement that follows if the chain fails entirely.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your S205 C 43 →

problems.title: M177 V8 Valve Cover Oil Leaks
problems.affectedModels: S205 AMG C 63, C 63 S (all M177.980 variants).
problems.rootCause: The hot-vee layout concentrates heat between the cylinder banks, accelerating gasket and seal degradation — valve cover oil leaks are the most common complaint, with oil seeping onto exhaust manifolds.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of oil smell or visible seepage around the valve covers, have the gaskets replaced immediately — an £800–£1,500 specialist repair is far cheaper than the £8,000+ replacement that follows if oil loss leads to bearing failure.

cta.label: Compare M177 engine prices for your S205 C 63 →

problems.title: OM654 AdBlue System Failures
problems.rootCause: The AdBlue (DEF) system uses NOx sensors and a heater that can fail — leading to warning lights, reduced power, and in some cases, the car refusing to start if the AdBlue system is not repaired.
problems.recommendation: On any OM654-powered S205 over 60,000 miles, have the AdBlue system inspected annually — a £500–£1,200 sensor replacement is cheap insurance against a £3,000+ engine replacement.

cta.label: Compare OM654 engine prices for your S205 C 220d →

replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the baseline figures — the S205 estate shares its MRA platform with the W205 saloon, and engine-bay access is consistent across the range. Costs are inherited from the EM data's Table A.
